candidate.description: Replace exact phrase matching with sliding 2-word phrases plus prefix-expanded individual tokens
candidate.rationale: Exact multi-word phrases rarely match conversation text. Generating all adjacent word pairs captures topical relationships while individual tokens with prefix expansion ensure recall. This avoids the zero-results problem while preserving positional signals.
candidate.expected_improvements: Multi-word topic queries like "autoresearch projects ranked" or "database connection problem" should match documents containing adjacent pairs ("autoresearch projects", "connection problem") even when the full phrase doesn't exist. Single-word queries gain prefix expansion.
candidate.unified_diff:
--- a/engine/server.py
+++ b/engine/server.py
@@ -146,15 +146,27 @@ def health():
 # =============================================================
 # QUERY FUNCTIONS — shared by REST endpoints and MCP tools
 # =============================================================
 
 def _auto_phrase(q: str) -> str:
-    """Wrap bare multi-token queries in quotes so BM25 ranks positional
-    phrase matches instead of bag-of-words token density. Callers passing
-    explicit FTS5 syntax (quotes, booleans, NEAR, prefix*, column filters,
-    grouping) pass through unchanged. Also makes hyphenated tokens work
-    without requiring callers to quote them themselves."""
+    """Generate sliding 2-word phrases combined with prefix-expanded tokens.
+    Balances phrase matching for adjacent pairs with flexibility of bag-of-words.
+    Callers passing explicit FTS5 syntax pass through unchanged."""
     if not q or any(c in q for c in '"*():^'):
         return q
     tokens = q.split()
     if any(op in tokens for op in ("AND", "OR", "NOT", "NEAR")):
         return q
-    return f'"{q}"'
+    
+    if len(tokens) == 1:
+        return f"{q}*" if len(q) >= 3 else q
+    
+    parts = []
+    # Sliding window of 2-word phrases
+    for i in range(len(tokens) - 1):
+        parts.append(f'"{tokens[i]} {tokens[i+1]}"')
+    
+    # Individual tokens with prefix expansion for terms >= 3 chars
+    for t in tokens:
+        if len(t) >= 3:
+            parts.append(f"{t}*")
+    
+    return " OR ".join(parts)

candidate.manifestation: A 15-person product agency has 4 employees using Claude for client briefs, 3 using ChatGPT for content, and 2 using Copilot for code. When the account lead asks "what did we decide about Client X's API scope?", the decision exists in Sarah's ChatGPT history from Tuesday—but no one else can access it, so the team re-litigates the same decision in a 45-minute Slack thread.
candidate.cost_model: For a 15-person agency where 10 people use AI tools daily and each person's private AI context contains 3 critical decisions weekly that others need: 10 people × 3 decisions × 15 min re-explaining × 50 weeks = 375 hours annually = $30,000 at $80/hour fully-loaded cost.
candidate.inevitability: Per-seat licenses (ChatGPT, Copilot) architecturally silo memory by user—one person's context is invisible to teammates. As AI adoption grows, fragmentation compounds: more tools × more seats × more private histories = exponentially more hiding places for critical context. Teams get 10% better individually but nothing compounds organizationally.
candidate.counter_narrative: "Just take better notes" assumes people remember which decisions happened in AI versus human conversations. In practice, rapid iteration happens in AI—5 refinement rounds in Claude before a single Slack message goes out—and those rounds vanish into one person's chat history, taking the reasoning and alternatives with them.
candidate.evidence_anchor: Atlan's context engineering research defines "missing context" as when "The AI has no memory of prior decisions"—manifesting organizationally when those decisions exist in a coworker's private chat history that per-seat licensing architecturally prevents sharing.

reasons.legibility: Vivid CS Manager scenario with concrete examples, but "context window" is technical jargon.
reasons.economic: Full cost chain: 8 people × 4 shifts × 72 outputs × 30% × 25 min = $24,480 annually.
reasons.inevitability: Frames contamination windows as structural and compounding with AI adoption across multiple surfaces.

candidate.definition: Context rot is when AI tools confidently act on outdated information still present in their context window—old pricing, deprecated features, departed team members—forcing rework when outputs are based on wrong assumptions embedded in stale context.
candidate.manifestation: A Customer Success Manager at a 50-person B2B SaaS company uses Claude for renewal proposals and escalation emails. After a February pricing update, their long-running Claude session still contains January pricing sheets for 6 weeks (until old content ages out of the context window). During this period, 30% of customer-facing documents contain wrong pricing or discount policies—often caught when customers reply confused, requiring apology and redo.
candidate.cost_model: 8-person CS team, each experiencing 4 major context shifts annually (pricing updates, product launches, re-orgs). In the 6 weeks following each shift, 30% of their 12 weekly customer-facing outputs contain stale-context errors, each requiring 25 minutes to detect and correct (customer feedback loop + redo). Total: 8 people × 4 shifts × 72 outputs × 30% × 25 min = 288 hours annually, or $24,480 at $85/hour.
candidate.inevitability: As AI handles more customer-facing work, every business change creates a contamination window where outputs are systematically wrong until stale context ages out. The more surfaces AI touches (pricing, features, team structure, policies), the more contamination windows overlap—making it structurally impossible to keep context current without constant manual intervention.
candidate.counter_narrative: "Just use a bigger context window" extends the contamination period, not shortens it. Atlan identified "stale, conflicting, or irrelevant context" as a core failure mode of context engineering—a 1M token window means January pricing persists through March, contaminating 3 months of outputs instead of 3 weeks.
candidate.evidence_anchor: Atlan's "What Is Context Engineering?" explicitly names stale context as one of four failure modes, and @shao_meng documented how "older irrelevant content starts to distract from current task" in extended sessions.

reasons.legibility: Cross-project contamination in plain language; healthcare/prosumer/enterprise examples immediately recognizable to any multi-product manager.
reasons.economic: Full cost chain: 18 specs/month × 60% contamination × 40 min × $60/hr = $5,160/yr per PM, scales to team.
reasons.inevitability: Quadratic growth (N products create N(N-1)/2 contamination pairs) with architectural trap: contamination vs. amnesia, no middle ground.

candidate.definition: Context rot is when AI tools mix context from unrelated projects in a shared conversation history, causing recommendations for Project A to be contaminated by constraints, priorities, or architectures from Project B.
candidate.manifestation: A Product Manager at a 35-person B2B SaaS company manages three products: a healthcare app (HIPAA-focused), a prosumer tool (speed-focused), and an enterprise platform (SSO/audit-focused). In a single Claude session spanning all three, they draft a prosumer feature spec on Wednesday—Claude suggests "encrypt all data at rest with customer-managed keys" (massive over-engineering borrowed from Monday's healthcare context). On Friday, drafting an enterprise spec, Claude suggests "use Google OAuth for SSO" (borrowed from Wednesday's prosumer context—enterprise customers demand SAML, not consumer OAuth). Each contaminated suggestion requires 40 minutes to catch, research the correct approach, and re-prompt.
candidate.cost_model: For a PM managing 3 products, creating 6 specs per product per month in mixed-context sessions: 18 specs/month, contamination in 60% (cross-project suggestion wrong for current context), 40 min per fix. 18 × 0.6 × 40 = 432 min/month = 86 hrs/year at $60/hr = $5,160/year per PM. For a 4-person product team: $20,640/year.
candidate.inevitability: The more products you manage with AI, the worse it gets—managing 4 products creates 6 cross-contamination pairs, 5 products creates 10 pairs (quadratic growth). Starting fresh sessions for each product eliminates contamination but forces re-teaching Claude your product spec format, prioritization framework, and technical constraints in every session—you lose cross-product learning and repeat onboarding overhead forever.
candidate.counter_narrative: "Just use separate chat sessions per project" eliminates cross-contamination but defeats the point of AI memory. In practice, PMs optimize for continuity—they stay in one session to avoid re-explaining their company's frameworks and constraints every time. Uncle Bob observed: "it remembers too much"—but the inverse (forgetting everything via fresh sessions) means re-onboarding Claude in every conversation, indefinitely.
candidate.evidence_anchor: Uncle Bob ("it remembers too much") and @shao__meng ("older irrelevant content starts to distract from current task")

reasons.legibility: Vivid engineer story with concrete numbers (180→5,200 lines), but CLAUDE.md and "temporal awareness" require slight insider context.
reasons.economic: Full cost calculation with specific numbers (12 min/week, 2.5 incidents, 108 hours/year) but doesn't convert to dollars.
reasons.inevitability: Structural append-only mechanism with evidence citation (@alxfazio) shows compounding degradation as team and codebase grow.

candidate.definition: Context rot is when AI instruction files accumulate faster than teams can prune them, mixing current architecture with abandoned features and deprecated code, until the AI can't distinguish active decisions from historical artifacts.
candidate.manifestation: A senior engineer at a 30-person SaaS company maintains a CLAUDE.md file for a 10-month-old API service that began with 180 lines of standards and now contains 5,200 lines after 7 months: deprecated Stripe webhooks, an abandoned message queue, outdated oncall rotation, two database schemas (one retired), and three conflicting caching strategies from successive refactors. Claude reads all 5,200 lines each session with no temporal awareness and confidently recommends the message queue removed four months ago. When the engineer debugs why the suggested code references non-existent infrastructure, they realize 40% of the file is historical artifacts the AI treats as current truth.
candidate.cost_model: For a 5-engineer team maintaining shared CLAUDE.md over 12 months: 12 min/week/engineer appending updates = 52 hours/year maintenance. After month 6 when the file exceeds 3,000 lines, stale instructions cause 2.5 debugging incidents/week/engineer requiring 20 min each to identify that the AI followed deprecated guidance = 5 engineers × 2.5 incidents × 20 min × 26 weeks = 108 hours/year wasted.
candidate.inevitability: File-based memory has no expiration mechanism—only append operations. Every architecture change, API update, and refactor adds context, but teams rarely delete "potentially useful" history. The longer the codebase evolves and the more engineers contribute, the faster files degrade into what @alxfazio termed "useless 6k line context rot," where every retrieval surfaces equal parts current truth and historical fiction.
candidate.counter_narrative: "Just maintain a CLAUDE.md file" feels like taking control—explicitly curating what the AI sees rather than trusting opaque memory systems. But @alxfazio documented the collapse: these files "turn into a useless 6k line context rot" because teams append decisions faster than they prune obsolete ones. The AI reads the entire file with no temporal awareness, so "switched to SQLite in June" and "use PostgreSQL in production" coexist as equally weighted facts, and whichever appears first wins.
candidate.evidence_anchor: @alxfazio, @unclebobmartin

reasons.legibility: Definition names failure (memory silos), behavior (re-teaching fundamentals), and cost (training tax) in plain language any VP recognizes.

reasons.inevitability: Architectural per-seat licensing guarantees silos scaling with headcount, but lacks deep practitioner evidence citations like dbreunig or Chroma.

candidate.definition: Context rot is when AI memory silos force every employee to independently re-teach company fundamentals—pricing, competitors, product details—creating a training tax that scales with headcount.
candidate.manifestation: A 12-person sales team uses Claude for proposal drafting and objection handling. Each AE starts fresh sessions 3× weekly (new deal, new thread, context limit hit) and spends the first 5 minutes re-explaining: our three pricing tiers, top 3 competitors, ICP characteristics, standard discount thresholds. The AI never "learns" company context—it resets per session and per person.
candidate.cost_model: 12 AEs × 3 teaching sessions/week × 5 min/session = 180 min/week = 156 hours/year. At $75/hour fully loaded cost = $11,700/year in pure re-onboarding time. Implicit cost is higher: proposals that should take 10 minutes take 15 because the AI doesn't know baseline context—adding 60 hours/year of work that AI was supposed to eliminate.
candidate.inevitability: Per-seat licensing (ChatGPT Plus, Claude Pro, Copilot) is the dominant AI business model, architecturally guaranteeing memory silos—your memory is not my memory. As adoption grows, the re-onboarding tax scales directly with headcount: 10 employees = 10× teaching, 100 employees = 100× teaching, with no compounding across the organization.
candidate.counter_narrative: Managed memory like Anthropic memory_stores only solves per-person retention, not cross-team knowledge. Each employee still maintains their own private memory, so 12 AEs still independently teach the same pricing tiers, competitor landscape, and product details 156 hours/year combined. Per-seat licensing architectures (ChatGPT Plus, Claude Pro, Copilot) silo memory by design—your memory is not my memory.
candidate.evidence_anchor: Ground truth post 3: "Everyone's AI is in a silo... They don't know your company, your pricing, your relationship history." Atlan identifies "missing context" as a core failure mode of context engineering.

reasons.legibility: Vivid DevOps scenario with concrete failures, clear language, but definition requires AI-tool familiarity to parse fully.
reasons.economic: Full cost chain with frequency, duration, rate, but hallucination rate jump (5%→35%) feels estimated not measured.
reasons.inevitability: Structural no-expiration mechanism, cites Chroma and @alxfazio, names compounding mechanism as adoption scales.

candidate.definition: Context rot is when file-based AI memory accumulates decisions without expiration, causing critical current requirements to drown in obsolete historical noise.
candidate.manifestation: A DevOps Engineer at a 40-person startup maintains a CLAUDE.md file in the infrastructure repo with deployment constraints and architecture decisions. After 8 weeks and 200+ appends, the file hits 4,500 lines. Claude now suggests using Heroku (migrated off in March) and Redis sessions (deprecated for JWT in April) because those decisions are buried in lines 340 and 890, drowning under recent additions—the engineer spends 20 minutes debugging configs that confidently implement obsolete architecture.
candidate.cost_model: 5-engineer platform team, each running 3 AI-assisted infrastructure tasks daily (configs, scripts, troubleshooting). In weeks 1-6 (CLAUDE.md <3000 lines), 5% of outputs hallucinate, costing 15 min/day team-wide to debug. In weeks 7+ (file >3000 lines), hallucination rate jumps to 35% as obsolete context drowns current constraints, costing 105 min/day. Delta: 90 min/day = 7.5 hours/week = 390 hours/year at $120/hour loaded cost = $46,800/year in rework caused by context bloat.
candidate.inevitability: As AI adoption grows, teams create more file-based context (CLAUDE.md, .cursorrules, .github/copilot.md) and append aggressively to capture institutional knowledge. These files have no expiration mechanism—everything persists equally—so the more you use them, the faster they bloat. Chroma Research showed degradation is discontinuous: accuracy drops sharply at certain context thresholds, and @alxfazio documented the endpoint: files that "turn into useless 6k line context rot."
candidate.counter_narrative: "Just prune the file regularly" assumes someone knows what's obsolete versus still relevant. In practice, requirements overlap—"we use PostgreSQL" (line 120, still true) coexists with "we use Heroku Postgres" (line 340, now false after Railway migration) and "connection pooling required" (line 890, still true but implementation changed)—so no single engineer can safely prune without risking deletion of a load-bearing constraint. @alxfazio named this exact failure: CLAUDE.md files that "turn into useless 6k line context rot."
candidate.evidence_anchor: @alxfazio

reasons.legibility: Vivid 12-person team scenario with concrete examples (Railway vs AWS, Postgres vs Turso), but definition front-loads technical file names.
reasons.economic: Complete cost chain with all multipliers (frequency, encounter rate, duration, rate) totaling $16,896/year; CFO could model this.
reasons.inevitability: Structural asymmetry (30-sec add vs 20-min delete audit) guarantees append-only behavior; cites @shao__meng; worsens as teams ship faster.

candidate.definition: Context rot is when file-based memory systems (CLAUDE.md, .cursorrules) accumulate stale architecture decisions and deprecated constraints faster than teams can prune them, causing AI tools to confidently suggest outdated approaches.
candidate.manifestation: A 12-person engineering team uses Cursor with a shared CLAUDE.md that grows from 400 lines to 5,800 over four months, mixing current architecture ("deploy to Railway") with deprecated decisions ("deploy to AWS," "use PostgreSQL" instead of current Turso). Engineers starting new sessions get confidently wrong suggestions—AWS scripts, Postgres migrations, deprecated imports—requiring 10 minutes to recognize, reject, and re-prompt.
candidate.cost_model: 12 engineers × 16 AI-assisted coding sessions/month × 40% encounter stale suggestions × 10 min rework each = 768 min/month = 154 hours/year × $110/hour = $16,896/year in direct costs. Doesn't count trust erosion: after repeated stale suggestions, engineers stop asking architecture questions and limit AI to autocomplete.
candidate.inevitability: As teams ship faster, CLAUDE.md becomes an append-only log—more decisions logged, more pivots creating deprecated-but-present context. Addition is instant (30-sec copy-paste); deletion is fraught (20-min audit, unclear what's still needed). @shao__meng observed "older irrelevant content starts to distract from current task"—file-based memory guarantees this by design.
candidate.counter_narrative: "Just keep the file updated" assumes teams prune as often as they add, but @alxfazio documented the opposite: files "turn into a useless 6k line context rot." The incentive structure ensures accumulation—adding context is insurance ("help the AI"), pruning is uncertain ROI. Atlan identified "stale, conflicting, or irrelevant context" as core failure modes; file-based memory produces all three simultaneously.
candidate.evidence_anchor: @alxfazio + Atlan

reasons.legibility: Vivid consulting scenario is recognizable, but definition sentence is somewhat abstract; clear language but not quite LinkedIn-headline crisp.

candidate.manifestation: A 20-person consulting firm buys ChatGPT Team licenses. Over 6 months, their senior consultant builds deep context about a key client's tech stack, competitors, and past decisions. reasons.legibility: Concrete persona and scenario (Head of Engineering, 10 weeks, 5-hour recovery) with vivid symptoms; 'tokens' term used but contextualized.
reasons.economic: Complete cost chain: 4 resets/year × 5 hours × $220/hour = $4,400 per leader, scaled to $13,200; CFO-ready ROI model.
reasons.inevitability: Cites @dbreunig 20% threshold evidence; frames as transformer architecture limitation; worsens as adoption shifts from ephemeral to quarters-long strategic sessions.

candidate.definition: Context rot is when AI context windows promise 1 million tokens but degrade catastrophically after 200,000, leaving 80% of the advertised capacity unusable and forcing users to reset sessions mid-project.
candidate.manifestation: A Head of Engineering at a 90-person SaaS company uses Claude Opus for architecture reviews and technical debt planning across a 3-month product cycle. By week 10, the session has 220,000 tokens (22% of the 1M window). Claude's architecture suggestions suddenly become generic, miss constraints from week 2, and contradict decisions from week 5. After 2 hours debugging the degradation, the Head of Engineering realizes the context window itself is broken and spends another 3 hours re-teaching 10 weeks of architecture context in a fresh session.
candidate.cost_model: Engineering leaders running quarters-long architecture sessions hit the 20% degradation threshold every 10-12 weeks: 4 resets/year × 5 hours per reset (2 hours debugging + 3 hours re-teaching) = 20 hours/year × $220/hour fully loaded = $4,400 per leader. For a company with 3 engineering managers: $13,200/year in reset tax, plus implicit cost of wrong architectural decisions made during the unnoticed degradation window (weeks 8-10).
candidate.inevitability: @dbreunig measured "SIGNIFICANT decrease in performance at tokens > 20% consumed on Opus 4.6—it degrades INSANELY, like the 1M context doesn't matter." As AI adoption shifts from ephemeral Q&A to quarters-long strategic sessions (architecture planning, customer research), every power user crosses the 20% threshold and discovers 80% of the window is unusable.
candidate.counter_narrative: "Just use a bigger context window" is the industry's answer (10M tokens!), but @dbreunig showed bigger windows delay degradation, not solve it. A 10M window degrading at 2M tokens is no better than a 1M window degrading at 200K. The problem is attention dispersion: transformer architectures can't maintain coherence across hundreds of thousands of tokens regardless of total capacity.
candidate.evidence_anchor: @dbreunig (20% degradation threshold on Claude Opus 1M window)

reasons.legibility: One-sentence definition naming what breaks (outdated product info), when (after updates), and cost (escalations) in plain English.

candidate.definition: Context rot is when AI tools retain outdated product information—old features, deprecated APIs, changed pricing—and confidently produce wrong answers that slip into customer communications, causing escalations and trust erosion.
candidate.manifestation: An 8-person support team at a B2B SaaS company uses AI to draft ticket responses referencing product docs and runbooks. After each 2-week sprint updates 10% of features, 3% of AI-drafted responses contain stale information (old API endpoints, deprecated workarounds, outdated policies). 60% caught by agents before sending (8 min rework each), 25% caught by customers (20 min to fix), 15% slip through requiring engineer debugging.
candidate.cost_model: 8 agents × 15 AI responses/day × 250 days = 30,000 responses/year. 3% stale rate = 900 wrong answers/year. Triage: 540 caught internally (72 hours agent time), 225 customer escalations (75 hours), 135 slip through (135 engineer hours). Total: 147 agent hours × $55 + 135 engineer hours × $110 = $22,935/year in direct rework costs, plus unmeasured customer churn risk from receiving incorrect technical information.
candidate.inevitability: As AI adoption spreads to customer-facing roles (support, sales, onboarding), stale context compounds: more people drafting responses × faster product velocity × no versioning mechanism = exponentially more wrong answers reaching customers. The cost scales with both team size and product update frequency, and current architectures provide no way to invalidate outdated memories across a team.
candidate.counter_narrative: "Just update the knowledge base more frequently" assumes AI context supports versioning and invalidation, but it doesn't—per-seat tools (ChatGPT, Claude) can't push centralized updates, file-based systems (CLAUDE.md) require manual edits that lag product changes, and managed memory (Anthropic memory_stores) is a black box with no invalidation API. Atlan's research explicitly names "stale context" as a core failure mode of current architectures; no amount of manual updating fixes the lack of version control at the memory layer.

candidate.definition: Context rot is when AI tools fail to isolate context between parallel workstreams, causing cross-customer data leakage when the same user manages multiple accounts in long-running conversation threads.
candidate.manifestation: A Customer Success Manager at a 100-person B2B SaaS company maintains long-running Claude threads for 40 enterprise accounts to preserve QBR discussions, expansion opportunities, and support history. When preparing Customer B's renewal analysis on Tuesday afternoon, Claude suggests Customer A's expansion play from Monday or misattributes Customer C's usage drop to Customer B—by turn 4-5 in any customer thread, cross-contamination appears. The CSM catches it, restarts, and spends 12 minutes re-explaining Customer B's context from scratch.
candidate.cost_model: 10 AI-assisted customer conversations/week, 30% contaminated by turn 4-5 (3/week). Each contamination costs 12 minutes (catch + restart + re-explain) = 36 min/week = 31 hours/year per CSM. At $65/hour × 5-person team = $10,075/year in restart time, plus implicit reputation cost: one leaked metric or misattributed churn risk in a live call destroys customer trust.
candidate.inevitability: As CS teams scale from occasional use to every customer interaction, contamination compounds: 40 accounts × 6-month histories × weekly touchpoints = thousands of tokens of irrelevant context per session. @shao__meng documented "older irrelevant content starts to distract from current task"—with 40 customers, every session has 39 competing sources of distraction. The more accounts per CSM and the longer the session histories, the worse the cross-pollination becomes.
candidate.counter_narrative: "Start a fresh conversation for each customer" eliminates contamination but forces the CSM to re-explain customer context every session, negating AI's efficiency value. Per-seat tools don't support multi-tenant context isolation, so users choose: maintain sessions and accept cross-contamination (@shao__meng: "older irrelevant content starts to distract from current task"), or reset sessions and lose longitudinal memory. The architecture makes both options fail.

candidate.counter_narrative: "Just copy-paste your notes into each session" assumes SEs curate context between back-to-back calls with perfect discipline and that text notes capture conversational nuance. @alxfazio documented what actually happens: notes "turn into a useless 6k line context rot" because teams append without pruning, and by the final proposal session the AI drowns in stale details from the first discovery call.
candidate.evidence_anchor: Spotify Engineering documented models "forgetting the original task after a few turns"—in multi-week sales cycles, this manifests as forgetting deal context across sessions, causing the AI to contradict its own prior outputs from earlier in the cycle.

reasons.legibility: DevOps-specific scenario is vivid but narrow; non-technical buyer outside infrastructure wouldn't recognize their own context rot pain.
reasons.economic: Complete cost chain: frequency × duration × rate = dollar impact; CFO could model ROI; verification tax is clever.
reasons.inevitability: Structural and evidence-backed (@dbreunig, @shao_meng); compounding within engineer's usage clear; doesn't extend to organizational cross-contamination.

candidate.definition: Context rot is when AI tools retain outdated infrastructure context—old configurations, deprecated commands, past troubleshooting decisions—and contaminate current incident response with dangerous suggestions that delay resolution.
candidate.manifestation: A DevOps engineer at a 50-person SaaS company uses Claude for Kubernetes troubleshooting across 30 incidents/year. After 4 months, AI has accumulated context from 2 cluster upgrades, 12 production incidents, and 25 config changes. When a new network issue appears, AI suggests kubectl commands with deprecated flags from the previous k8s version or references a load balancer topology that was replaced in March, forcing the engineer to debug the AI's obsolete guidance before addressing the actual incident.
candidate.cost_model: 30 incidents/year per engineer; degradation threshold (@dbreunig: 20% window) hit at 4 months; 8 months/year operating degraded; 20 incidents in degraded state; 50% contaminated = 10 incidents; 10 incidents × 2.5 hours debugging wrong suggestions = 25 hours at $120/hr = $3,000; 2 escalations to SEV-2 from delay = 8 hours × $200/hr = $1,600; verification tax (learned distrust): 30 incidents × 8 AI suggestions × 3 min validation = 12 hours × $120 = $1,440; total per engineer: $6,040/year; 4-person DevOps team: $24,160/year.
candidate.inevitability: Every infrastructure change—version upgrades, config updates, architecture refactors—adds context that will contaminate future troubleshooting. @dbreunig measured "INSANELY" degraded performance at just 20% of context consumed, and @shao_meng documented "older irrelevant content starts to distract from current task." The more incidents you resolve with AI assistance, the more obsolete technical context accumulates, and AI tools have no concept of "this config expired when we upgraded Kubernetes in March."
candidate.counter_narrative: "Just start a fresh session for each incident" assumes perfect discipline during 3am production outages and ignores that even intentional persistence accumulates rot—@alxfazio documented file-based memory "turns into useless 6k line context rot." Spotify Engineering showed models "forgetting the original task after a few turns" even within single sessions, so fragmentation trades accumulation failures for amnesia failures without solving contamination.
candidate.evidence_anchor: @dbreunig (20% threshold degradation), @shao_meng (irrelevant content distraction), @alxfazio (context rot in managed files), Spotify Engineering (task forgetting)

reasons.legibility: Vivid technical PM scenario with concrete timeline and failure modes, though "tokens" and "AI attention" introduce jargon.

reasons.inevitability: Frames attention dispersion as architectural ceiling worsening with deeper AI use; cites Chroma/dbreunig/shao__meng on discontinuous degradation.

candidate.definition: Context rot is when AI attention degrades catastrophically as conversation history grows, causing the model to lose focus on current tasks regardless of prompt quality or user discipline.
candidate.manifestation: A technical product manager uses Claude for a 6-week feature specification, crafting careful prompts to maintain context. By week 4 (180K tokens), Claude starts citing deprecated week-1 designs instead of current architecture; by week 6, it forgets the original task entirely, forcing a full session restart.
candidate.cost_model: 3 projects/quarter × 2 restarts × 6 hours reconstruction = 144 hours/year at $110 = $15,840; plus 12 deprecated-design incidents × 3 hours rework at $125 = $4,500. Total: $20,340 annual.
candidate.inevitability: Attention dispersion is architectural—@shao__meng documented that "older irrelevant content starts to distract from current task" once context accumulates, and no prompt can override the model's attention mechanism scanning 200K+ tokens. Chroma Research proved degradation is discontinuous: once you cross the threshold, recovery is impossible. As AI use deepens and projects lengthen, every team hits this performance cliff.
candidate.counter_narrative: "Just write better prompts" assumes context rot stems from poor phrasing, but @dbreunig measured "SIGNIFICANT decrease in performance at tokens > 20% consumed" regardless of prompt structure. Chroma Research proved degradation is discontinuous—it's an attention architecture failure, not a user skill gap. Even perfect prompts can't prevent @shao__meng's observation: "older irrelevant content starts to distract from current task."
candidate.evidence_anchor: @dbreunig (degradation >20% tokens), @shao__meng (attention dispersion), and Chroma Research (discontinuous thresholds).

candidate.definition: Context rot is when AI tools accumulate conversational debris from previous tasks without project boundaries, causing yesterday's unrelated work to contaminate today's sessions—the more you use it successfully, the worse it performs.
candidate.manifestation: A Product Manager at a SaaS company uses Claude for 4 parallel feature tracks—enterprise API, self-serve onboarding, billing redesign, mobile app. By month 3, Claude starts cross-contaminating: suggesting self-serve UI patterns in the enterprise API spec, citing mobile constraints in the billing redesign, or referencing competitive intel from the wrong market segment.
candidate.cost_model: PM producing 20 AI-assisted docs/quarter (80/year) across 4 product tracks. 15% cross-contamination rate = 12 contaminated docs/year. Caught internally: 8 docs × 1.5 hours rework at $110 = $1,320. Escape to stakeholders: 4 docs × 1 correction meeting × 5 people × 1.5 hours × $130 blended = $3,900. 2 contaminated specs reach engineering = 2 × 3 hours × 4 engineers × $125 = $3,000. Total: $8,220/year per PM.
candidate.inevitability: Every AI-assisted document adds 8K-12K tokens of context. A PM producing 80 docs/year accumulates 640K-960K tokens by year-end—well past @dbreunig's 200K degradation threshold (20% of 1M window), which hits in Q2 and persists through Q4. More AI productivity = faster accumulation = earlier contamination. @shao__meng documented the mechanism: "older irrelevant content starts to distract from current task"—the architecture treats all context equally, so can't distinguish project A's relevant history from project B's noise.
candidate.counter_narrative: "Just start a new session for each project" assumes work arrives in clean silos, but real workflows have intentional cross-references—designing project B's API integration requires constraints from project A, or UX consistency across products. @unclebobmartin named the paradox: "it remembers too much"—but forgetting everything breaks legitimate cross-project continuity. The architecture can't distinguish useful cross-references from contaminating noise.
candidate.evidence_anchor: @shao__meng (attention dispersion from older content) and @unclebobmartin (remembers too much)

candidate.definition: Context rot is when AI-generated onboarding materials reference deprecated infrastructure—outdated tools, changed workflows, migrated systems—causing new hires to follow wrong procedures that block their progress and require senior engineer interrupts to correct.
candidate.manifestation: A 50-person engineering team onboards 3 engineers monthly using AI to generate setup guides for local environment, deploy pipeline, code review, monitoring, and oncall. After 6 months of infrastructure evolution (3 tool migrations, 4 process changes), 25% of AI-generated steps reference systems that no longer exist, sending new hires down dead-end paths.

Unlike product knowledge (updated in documented releases), infrastructure changes are distributed and undocumented—invisible until a new hire hits a dead end.

Spotify Engineering observed tools "forgetting the original task after a few turns"—even perfect docs don't prevent context from going stale within a single session.

reasons.legibility: Names a concrete support scenario (shift handoffs, customer repeating symptoms) that a VP of Ops would immediately recognize.
reasons.economic: Full cost chain with frequency, duration, rate, and secondary escalation costs—CFO could build ROI model directly.

candidate.definition: Context rot is when support escalations fragment across agent shifts and AI sessions, causing each new agent's AI to restart diagnostics from scratch while the customer repeats their environment and symptoms multiple times.
candidate.manifestation: A B2B SaaS company's technical support team (6 agents across 3 shifts) handles tier-2 escalations using per-seat Claude licenses for log analysis and debugging. A customer's database performance issue arrives Monday morning; first agent uses Claude to analyze logs and suspects connection pooling. Evening shift agent opens a fresh Claude session, re-uploads logs, and Claude suggests query optimization instead—contradicting the morning's analysis. Next morning's agent starts over; Claude now recommends indexing. The customer has uploaded their config three times and explained the same symptoms to three different humans and three different AIs with no memory of each other.
candidate.cost_model: 15 tier-2 escalations/month, 60% span multiple shifts (9/month). Each shift handoff wastes 25 minutes (agent reads ticket + re-explains to AI + AI re-analyzes). Average 2 handoffs per case = 9 × 2 × 25 min = 450 min/month = 90 hours/year at $65/hour = $5,850 in duplicated diagnostics. Plus: 30% of multi-shift cases (32/year) generate customer friction, 15% escalate to account manager (5 cases × 2 hours PM time at $110) = $1,100. Total: $6,950/year for a 6-person team.
candidate.inevitability: Every per-seat license creates an independent memory silo. As companies add shift coverage (evenings, weekends, follow-the-sun), handoff frequency increases—a 24/7 support model means every tier-2 case crosses minimum 2 shift boundaries, requiring 3+ disconnected AI sessions. More coverage = more fragmentation.
candidate.counter_narrative: "Just document everything in the ticket" assumes ticket notes capture the AI's diagnostic reasoning and that agents read full history before starting their AI session. But Spotify Engineering documented that even with notes, models "forget the original task after a few turns"—the AI starts fresh regardless of written handoff, and suggests contradictory diagnostic paths because it has no memory of previous agents' AI-assisted analysis.
candidate.evidence_anchor: Spotify Engineering (production system task forgetting)

candidate.definition: Context rot is when shared AI context files become unmaintained accumulation sinks where every addition is permanent but nothing is pruned, turning team knowledge bases into noise repositories that contaminate every session.
candidate.manifestation: A 12-person engineering team maintains a shared CLAUDE.md file in their monorepo as 'Claude's project memory'—each engineer adds deployment notes, architecture decisions, API patterns, and debugging solutions. After 8 months, the file is 6,400 lines covering 47 features, 3 deprecated services, 2 migrated cloud providers, and 15 resolved-but-documented incidents. Claude spends the first 30 seconds of every session parsing irrelevant context before doing any work.
candidate.cost_model: 12 engineers × 4 AI sessions/day × 2 min attention tax from bloated context (parsing + correcting stale info) = 96 min/day = 8 hours/week = 416 hours/year at $115/hour = $47,840. Plus: 8 stale-context incidents/year × 3 hours rework at $115 = $2,760. Total: $50,600 annually for a 12-person team.
candidate.inevitability: File-based memory can only accumulate—there's no automated expiration or relevance scoring. As team size grows (more contributors) and project lifespan extends (more historical decisions), signal-to-noise ratio degrades as O(team_size × project_age). More AI adoption = more contributors writing to shared files = exponentially faster rot.
candidate.counter_narrative: "Just maintain a shared CLAUDE.md file" is the default pattern in repositories, but @alxfazio observed it degrades into "useless 6k line context rot" because files accumulate without expiration—teams add context but never prune stale entries. @unclebobmartin explains the mechanism: "it remembers too much"—retention without curation is the failure mode, not a feature.
candidate.evidence_anchor: @alxfazio and @unclebobmartin (Uncle Bob)

candidate.definition: Context rot is when AI tools can't distinguish current decisions from superseded ones, treating 6-week-old constraints as equally valid as this morning's pivot.

In week 1 they decide 'Target SMBs at $10/month,' then in week 5 pivot to 'Enterprise at $2K/month.' By week 8, Claude randomly alternates between SMB and enterprise framing because it weights 6-week-old decisions equally with this week's pivot.

candidate.definition: Context rot is when AI tools accumulate product history across rapid iteration cycles, causing customer-facing materials to cite deprecated features, sunset integrations, or superseded capabilities as if they're current.
candidate.manifestation: A Sales Enablement Manager at a Series B SaaS company (200 employees, $25M ARR) uses Claude daily to draft proposals, one-pagers, and ROI calculators; the product team ships updates every 2 weeks and pricing changes quarterly. By month 7, AI-generated proposals randomly cite "real-time Salesforce sync" (deprecated to batch-only in March), "free 10GB storage" (changed to 5GB in May), and "99.99% SLA" (downgraded to 99.9% in April). An enterprise prospect's technical team tests the real-time sync claim, discovers it's batch-only, and the $180K deal stalls for 3 weeks.
candidate.cost_model: 720 proposals/year, 25% stale after 6 months (180/year): 120 caught internally cost 144 hours rework × $85 = $12,240; 60 reach customers causing 170 hours delays/clarifications × $125 = $21,250 plus 5 damaged deals × $120K × 8% = $48,000. Total: $81,490/year.
candidate.inevitability: Product velocity and AI adoption are both accelerating—faster ship cycles mean more features to deprecate, more AI-generated materials mean more surfaces for stale claims to hide. Per-seat AI tools isolate each user's context, so when Engineering announces "Salesforce sync is now batch-only," the Sales Enablement Manager's AI doesn't know unless manually told. Atlan's research identifies stale context as one of four core failure modes of context engineering—it's architectural, not fixable by user discipline.
candidate.counter_narrative: "Just maintain a single source of truth document and reference it" assumes the AI will prioritize the latest doc over accumulated history, but Uncle Bob observed "one of the problems with a big context window is that it remembers too much"—old feature descriptions don't disappear when you add a new doc. @dbreunig measured "SIGNIFICANT decrease in performance at tokens > 20% consumed," meaning the 200K-token window fills with months of old proposals and product discussions, drowning current specs.

candidate.definition: Context rot is when adding more context capacity accelerates rather than prevents degradation, because models lose coherence from attention dispersion, not memory exhaustion.
candidate.manifestation: An Engineering Manager at a 200-person startup uses Claude Opus 4.6 (1M-token window) for architectural decision records across 5 platform teams, consolidating all design discussions into one conversation to enable cross-team pattern recognition. At 210K tokens (21% of capacity), Claude starts recommending Team A's database patterns when advising Team D's caching design, resurrects authentication approaches rejected 3 weeks prior, and cross-pollinates unrelated microservice constraints—despite 79% of the window still empty.

Degradation starts at 200K tokens (week 7). Weeks 7-13: 35% of 21 decisions require re-validation against team-specific constraints (7.4 decisions × 3 hours = 22.2 hours × $160/hr = $3,552/quarter). Contamination cost: 3 teams/quarter implement cross-pollinated patterns requiring later refactor = 3 × 12 hours × $160 = $5,760. Total: $9,312/quarter = $37,248/year.
candidate.inevitability: Vendors actively encourage longer conversations to "maximize context," causing users to consolidate workflows into single threads. dbreunig measured degradation starting at 20% of Opus 4.6's 1M window—functionally 200K tokens, not 1M. As AI adoption grows, practitioners follow best practices to "keep the AI in the loop," accumulating tokens faster and hitting thresholds earlier. Chroma Research proved degradation is discontinuous—sharp drops at specific thresholds—meaning bigger windows don't defer the problem, they just create larger unusable buffer zones.
candidate.counter_narrative: "Just use a bigger context window" assumes degradation stems from capacity constraints solvable by adding tokens, but dbreunig measured "SIGNIFICANT decrease in performance at tokens > 20% consumed on Opus 4.6. It degrades INSANELY, like the 1M context doesn't matter"—meaning 800K tokens are architectural overhead, not usable capacity. Chroma Research confirmed degradation is discontinuous: models hit sharp accuracy cliffs at thresholds, not gradual decline. Uncle Bob named the mechanism: "One of the problems with a big context window is that it remembers too much"—more capacity enables more noise accumulation, structurally worsening the problem bigger windows claim to solve.
candidate.evidence_anchor: @dbreunig (20% degradation threshold on 1M window), Chroma Research (discontinuous accuracy drops), Uncle Bob (remembers too much)

candidate.definition: Context rot is when teams cannot recover why decisions were made because the AI-assisted exploration that led to them fragments across inaccessible individual sessions.
candidate.manifestation: A VP of Engineering at a 300-person startup revisits a 6-month-old decision to consolidate microservices into a modular monolith. The staff engineer who led the evaluation used Claude across 15 sessions exploring trade-offs, but those conversations are locked in personal chat history. New Claude sessions give generic architecture advice, missing the team's specific constraints (6-person SRE team, $40K/month AWS costs, 3-week deployment cycles). A 4-hour reconstruction meeting with 6 senior engineers is scheduled.
candidate.cost_model: 18 major technical decisions/year, 50% require later review (9/year), 65% lost rationale needing reconstruction (5.85 meetings/year). Each meeting: 4 hours × 6 engineers × $125/hour = $3,000. Annual cost: $17,550 in reconstruction. Additionally, 2 decisions/year proceed without understanding original constraints, causing 60 hours architectural rework × $125 = $7,500. Total: $25,050/year per engineering org.
candidate.inevitability: Per-seat licensing (ChatGPT, Copilot) silos memory by design - Post 3: "Claude on one person's laptop. ChatGPT on another's" creating "everyone's AI is in a silo." As teams scale and turn over, decision-makers leave while their AI conversations remain inaccessible. A 100-person company at 15% annual turnover loses 15 decision-makers' context libraries per year. More AI-assisted decision-making = more institutional knowledge locked in personal accounts, compounding as adoption grows.
candidate.counter_narrative: "Just document decisions in ADRs or wiki pages" assumes teams transcribe not only conclusions but the full exploration - dismissed alternatives, specific constraints, trade-off reasoning discussed with AI. In practice, ADRs capture final decisions, but the context needed to intelligently revisit them (why alternative B failed, what budget/team/market factors narrowed options) remains siloed. Post 3: "They don't know your company, your pricing, your relationship history, or what happened on last week's call."
candidate.evidence_anchor: Post 3 (shared brain for teams)

candidate.definition: Context rot is the progressive degradation of AI effectiveness within a single conversation as message history accumulates, causing the model to lose focus on current tasks while retrieving irrelevant details from earlier in the session.

candidate.definition: Context rot is when organizational AI adoption fragments institutional knowledge across isolated per-user subscriptions, preventing learning from compounding and causing teams to redundantly re-solve problems already solved by colleagues.

candidate.rationale: The `claude mcp add` command exits 0 even when registration fails due to read-only config, old Claude version, or permissions issues. This patch detects the silent failure immediately and exits with an actionable error before the user believes install succeeded.
candidate.expected_impact: First-run users with read-only ~/.claude/ or old Claude Code versions will see a clear error at install time instead of finishing install, opening Claude Code, and discovering search_memory doesn't exist.
candidate.unified_diff:
--- a/installer/setup.sh
+++ b/installer/setup.sh
@@ -94,7 +94,16 @@ fi
 claude mcp add -s user --transport http \
   cctx-memory "$CCTX_URL/mcp/" \
   --header "Authorization: Bearer $CCTX_TOKEN"
 
+# Verify MCP server was actually registered
+VERIFIED=$(claude mcp list 2>/dev/null | grep -c '^cctx-memory:' || true)
+if [ "$VERIFIED" -eq 0 ]; then
+  echo "  ERROR: MCP server registration failed."
+  echo "  'claude mcp add' exited successfully but the server is not in 'claude mcp list'."
+  echo "  Common causes: ~/.claude/ is read-only, Claude Code version too old, or permissions issue."
+  exit 1
+fi
+
 echo "  Added MCP server: cctx-memory (user scope)"

candidate.description: Distinguish HTTP error types (401/404/502) in API probe and show response body preview when JSON parsing fails.
candidate.rationale: F2 and F8 cause users to retry blindly because the error doesn't say whether the token is bad, the URL is wrong, or the server is cold-starting; showing HTTP codes and response previews gives the exact next action.
candidate.expected_impact: First-run users with wrong tokens (401) or malformed URLs (404) see actionable errors instead of retrying setup with the same bad values; Railway users hitting 502 during cold start get an automatic retry instead of abandoning install.
candidate.unified_diff:
--- a/installer/setup.sh
+++ b/installer/setup.sh
@@ -69,24 +69,44 @@ fi
 CLAUDE_VERSION=$(claude --version 2>/dev/null || echo "unknown")
 echo "  Found: claude $CLAUDE_VERSION"

-STATS=$(curl -sf --connect-timeout 5 --max-time 10 \
+RESPONSE=$(curl -s --connect-timeout 5 --max-time 20 -w '\n%{http_code}' \
   -H "Authorization: Bearer $CCTX_TOKEN" \
   "$CCTX_URL/api/stats" 2>/dev/null) || {
-  echo "  ERROR: Could not reach $CCTX_URL/api/stats"
-  echo "  Check your --url and --token values."
+  echo "  ERROR: Connection failed to $CCTX_URL/api/stats"
+  echo "  Check your network connection and --url value."
   exit 1
 }
+
+HTTP_CODE=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | tail -n1)
+STATS=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | sed '$d')
+
+if [ "$HTTP_CODE" = "401" ]; then
+  echo "  ERROR: Unauthorized (HTTP 401)"
+  echo "  Your --token is invalid or expired. Get a new token from 'npx cctx deploy'."
+  exit 1
+elif [ "$HTTP_CODE" = "404" ]; then
+  echo "  ERROR: Not found (HTTP 404)"
+  echo "  Check your --url — the path should end at the domain (no /api or /mcp)."

+  echo "  Server is starting (HTTP $HTTP_CODE) — retrying in 5s..."

+

candidate.description: Detect when setup re-runs with different credentials and require Claude Code restart instead of claiming none is needed.

candidate.expected_impact: Users redeploying to new Railway instances no longer hit silent 401s; reduces support burden for "I re-ran setup but nothing works" cases.

candidate.description: Parse HTTP status codes from API connection probe to provide specific error guidance.
candidate.expected_impact: First-run users with wrong --token or --url see specific guidance (401→token, 404→URL format, 502/503→cold start), reducing retry attempts from 2-3 to 1.
candidate.rationale: Turns opaque "Could not reach" into actionable diagnostics by capturing HTTP status codes: 401 indicates bad token, 404 indicates wrong URL path, 502/503 indicates Railway cold start. User knows exactly which credential to fix instead of guessing both.
candidate.unified_diff:
--- a/installer/setup.sh
+++ b/installer/setup.sh
@@ -72,13 +72,21 @@ echo "  Found: claude $CLAUDE_VERSION"
 
 # --- Step 2: Test API connection ---
 echo "[2/6] Testing API connection..."
-STATS=$(curl -sf --connect-timeout 5 --max-time 10 \
+RESP=$(curl -sf --connect-timeout 5 --max-time 10 -w '\n%{http_code}' \
   -H "Authorization: Bearer $CCTX_TOKEN" \
-  "$CCTX_URL/api/stats" 2>/dev/null) || {
-  echo "  ERROR: Could not reach $CCTX_URL/api/stats"
-  echo "  Check your --url and --token values."
+  "$CCTX_URL/api/stats" 2>&1)
+CODE=$(echo "$RESP" | tail -1)
+
+if [ "$CODE" != "200" ]; then
+  case "$CODE" in
+    401) echo "  ERROR: Authentication failed (HTTP 401) — check --token" ;;
+    404) echo "  ERROR: Endpoint not found (HTTP 404) — verify --url format" ;;
+    502|503) echo "  ERROR: Server unavailable (HTTP $CODE) — Railway cold start, wait 30s" ;;
+    *) echo "  ERROR: Cannot connect — check --url, DNS, or network" ;;
+  esac
   exit 1
 fi
+STATS=$(echo "$RESP" | head -n-1)
 MSG_COUNT=$(echo "$STATS" | python3 -c "
 import sys, json
 try:

--- a/installer/setup.sh
+++ b/installer/setup.sh
@@ -101,6 +101,13 @@

   cctx-memory "$CCTX_URL/mcp/" \
   --header "Authorization: Bearer $CCTX_TOKEN"
+
+# Verify MCP server was actually registered (claude mcp add exits 0 even on failure)
+if ! claude mcp list 2>/dev/null | grep -q '^cctx-memory:'; then
+  echo "  ERROR: MCP server registration failed."
+  echo "  Check: claude mcp list — if empty, ~/.claude/ may be read-only or Claude Code outdated."
+  exit 1
+fi
 
 echo "  Added MCP server: cctx-memory (user scope)"

reasons.time_to_value: Detects F4 misconfig at install-time with actionable diagnostic, eliminating one retry cycle for read-only directories or outdated Claude Code.
reasons.failure_mode_coverage: Cites F4 and handles primary triggers (read-only directories, outdated Claude Code) but may miss project-scope conflict edge case.
reasons.error_craftsmanship: Two-line ERROR with two-space indent, names exact failed check and exact next command, zero marketing language, matches existing style.

candidate.description: Detects when claude mcp add exits 0 without actually registering the MCP server.
candidate.rationale: Claude Code's mcp add command returns exit code 0 even when registration fails due to read-only ~/.claude/ directories, outdated Claude Code versions, or project-scope conflicts. This guard verifies the server appears in mcp list before declaring success.
candidate.expected_impact: Users with read-only ~/.claude/ or outdated Claude Code get an actionable error at install-time instead of discovering broken MCP tools later, eliminating one retry cycle and shortening time-to-working-memory by 10-15 minutes.

reasons.guard_pattern_fidelity: Clean boolean check with two-line ERROR and exit 1 in seven lines, reuses bash regex idiom appropriately for pattern validation.

reasons.failure_mode_coverage: Valid new mode with specific triggers (whitespace, dollar sign) causing auth failures, but lacks empirical evidence users encounter this.

candidate.description: Rejects tokens containing whitespace or $ that would break the Bearer header or cause shell interpolation.

candidate.error_message: ERROR: Token contains whitespace or $ character.
candidate.expected_impact: Users who accidentally paste malformed tokens from terminal line wrapping or clipboard corruption get an immediate actionable error instead of a misleading API connection failure, eliminating one full retry cycle.
